title: Offshore Lands Authorities Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Energy and Mineral Resources.
plain_summary: This bill cancels eight orders made by presidents that stopped oil and gas companies from drilling in certain ocean areas. It also creates new rules that limit how much power future presidents have to block drilling in offshore waters. Under the new rules, presidents can only withdraw smaller areas for shorter amounts of time, and they must do detailed studies first. Congress will also get the chance to vote against any presidential order that tries to stop offshore drilling.
subjects: Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Offshore Lands Authorities Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Nullification of Presidential Withdrawals of Unleased Offshore Land
sections.text: The following presidential orders that took offshore land away from oil and gas leasing will no longer have any power or effect. First, the Presidential Memorandum from December twentieth two thousand sixteen about withdrawing parts of the Arctic Outer Continental Shelf from mineral leasing in the Chukchi Sea Planning Area and the Beaufort Sea Planning Area. Second, the Presidential Memorandum from December sixteenth two thousand fourteen about withdrawing the North Aleutian Basin Planning Area from leasing. Third, Section three of Executive Order thirteen thousand seven hundred fifty four about Northern Bering Sea climate resilience. Fourth, Section four part b of Executive Order thirteen thousand nine hundred ninety about bringing back Executive Order thirteen thousand seven hundred fifty four and the Presidential Memorandum from December twentieth two thousand sixteen. Fifth, the Presidential Memorandum from March thirteenth two thousand twenty three about withdrawing parts of the United States Arctic Coast from oil or gas leasing in the Beaufort Planning Area. Sixth, the Presidential Memorandum from December twentieth two thousand sixteen about withdrawing Atlantic Coast canyon areas from mineral leasing. Seventh, the Presidential Memorandum from January sixth two thousand twenty five about withdrawing Gulf of Mexico Atlantic and Pacific areas from oil or natural gas leasing. Eighth, the Presidential Memorandum from January sixth two thousand twenty five about withdrawing the Northern Bering Sea Climate Resilience Area from oil or natural gas leasing.

sections.title: Section 3  Limitation of Authority of the President to Withdraw Unleased Offshore Lands
sections.text: This section changes Section twelve part a of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act. The current law says the President can withdraw unleased lands from the outer Continental Shelf. The changes add new rules. First, except for the new limits in parts two and three, the President can still withdraw unleased lands from the outer Continental Shelf. Starting when this law takes effect, the President must send any withdrawal to the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives. Second, there are new limitations. A withdrawal cannot cover more than one hundred fifty thousand acres total or be connected to any other withdrawal. A withdrawal cannot last longer than twenty years. No President can withdraw more than five hundred thousand acres total without getting approval from Congress. Third, the President must complete certain assessments before making a withdrawal. The Secretary must complete a detailed study of the mineral resources in the area during the five years before the withdrawal. The Secretary must work with other department heads to assess the economic energy and national security value of the minerals found. The Secretary must study how much money the federal government states and special funds will lose from the withdrawal. The Secretary must send a report with all these findings to specific committees in the House and Senate. Fourth, there is a process for Congress to disapprove a withdrawal. Congress can pass a joint resolution that says it disapproves the withdrawal and the withdrawal will have no power. The resolution gets referred to the right committees. If a Senate committee does not report the resolution within twenty calendar days thirty Senators can petition to move it forward. There are special rules for how the Senate must consider the resolution including limited debate time of ten hours split equally between supporters and opponents. No amendments are allowed and voting happens right after debate. If both houses pass different versions they follow special procedures to resolve differences. If the President vetoes the resolution the Senate gets ten hours of debate split equally. Congress passes these rules as part of its constitutional power to make rules for itself. If Congress passes a disapproval resolution the withdrawal cannot take effect or continue. The President cannot reissue the same or very similar withdrawal unless Congress specifically authorizes it by law. Courts cannot review any decisions made under this process. Any agency action subject to these disapproval procedures must be submitted to Congress with the text a summary and the date it was taken. The submission date is when it reaches both the President of the Senate and Speaker of the House or when it becomes publicly available whichever is later. The disapproval process starts when the action is properly submitted to Congress. The President of the Senate and Speaker of the House must publish notice of the submission in the Congressional Record. Fifth, the President cannot make a withdrawal that conflicts with areas scheduled for lease sales under an approved oil and gas leasing program under Section eighteen.
